author: kevaundray
content: Checking! I think preliminary results with exex is good, but we don't want to use this to base our final results off of because its pretty reth specific

author: kevaundray
content: For the rewind penalty, I would expect clients to have implemented some sort of cache/storage for this -- ie whenever a block is validated, they would also generate the Execution Witness in place and save it somewhere -- this is probably not too bad to implement I think <@944007601400741989> ?

author: taulepton_
content: Which part is reth specific? You mean the database lookup latency?

author: taulepton_
content: We already have this implemented in the reth witness indexer

author: kevaundray
content: Does enginePayloadWithWitness serialize twice? ie once for engine API to send to the CL and then another to get it from the CL?

author: developeruche
content: Yeah... I think this would be important to have. Though the draw down was not much (in latency), but some client could support rewind of block depth > 64

author: kevaundray
content: Ah I meant exex as concept -- my thinking is that these optimizations would be in the clients (unless they are too invasive, but not sure that has been the case)

author: kevaundray
content: They probably only need to support rewinding upto N blocks where N is how deep of a reorg they can do?

author: taulepton_
content: Agreed I think it should be inside the core client. In that case, it would be more performant.

author: taulepton_
content: I think we should add custom pruning functionality as is done with most other archived storage items. That's how we've done it in the reth witness indexer.

author: kevaundray
content: You mean like allow someone to specify whether they want to keep only the first 10 witnesses?

author: developeruche
content: Yeah... that's what it seems (in RETH and ETHREX). Geth errors and becomes stall

author: taulepton_
content: exactly, or maybe it could be based on some rpc invocation to prune all witnesses below block x etc.

author: taulepton_
content: But clients will probably configure based on their user requirements.

author: kevaundray
content: Makes sense!

author: kevaundray
content:
I'm trying to get an understanding of the results from first principles.

With the engine_newPayload + debug_executionWitness route:

- The client would need to execute the block twice
- The second execution currently also involves rewinding (but should be minor if we call debug_executionWitness immediately after)
- This also involves two serialization roundtrips for each call

Does that sound correct?

author: developeruche
content: yes... all correct

author: kevaundray
content: Do you know where I can find a description of engine_newPayloadWithWitness?

author: developeruche
content: Which client?

author: kevaundray
content: Ah mainly just a general description

author: developeruche
content: I still named it `engine_newPayloadV3` and `engine_newPayloadV4` just change the internal logic

author: kevaundray
content: Is it just an EL endpoint that remove the serialization overhead of debug_executionWitness ?

author: taulepton_
content: I think the key takeaway, in my opinion, is that JSON-RPC is a no-go. I think it's still possible that `engine_newPayloadWithWitness` could be fine if we had more efficient transport.

author: developeruche
content: Yeah, and also re-execution as the witness is sent immediately execution is done... (most client have an efficient way for doing this)

author: kevaundray
content:
> JSON-RPC is a no-go.
Yeah agree 😄

author: kevaundray
content: How would the prover get the witness with engine_newPayloadWithWitness?

author: taulepton_
content: It would have to be sent in the request_proofs payload body

author: kevaundray
content: My understanding is that thats a method the CL calls on the EL, so is the idea here that the prover calls engine_newPayloadWithWitness as opposed to the CL?

author: taulepton_
content: I think the CL would call engine_newPayloadWithWitness when doing payload validation and then include the witness in the request_proofs call to the proof node?

author: developeruche
content: from the CL, I think... we kinda talk on this based on the propose design from our early discussion [here](https://cdn.discordapp.com/attachments/1463293742457815329/1463297528052191315/image.png?ex=69a2c1c5&is=69a17045&hm=3adf31b237c0ddc3bfb59a5ed4cb315fece6fddc50b95bc0d2a8c1c3b45816fa&_)

author: kevaundray
content:
Ah with this, I think newPayload + debug_executionWitness would have never been reasonable?

newPayload + debug_executionWitness would have only been okay if the prover was the one calling debug_executionWitness I think

author: developeruche
content: yeah

author: kevaundray
content: The previous design had the prover call debug_executionWitness which is one reason why EWS is a bit annoying to setup since we need to listen for new blocks from the CL and then get the witness from the EL

author: kevaundray
content: <@402080271563751435> I'm wondering how we will convince folks to change the engine api from json rpc 😄

author: taulepton_
content: In some sense, they already have

author: taulepton_
content: If I'm not mistaken engine api already supports IPC and web socket

author: taulepton_
content: We would not be proposing deprecation of pre-existing solutions just support for a new more efficient transport mode

author: kevaundray
content: Maybe we also use a different namespace zk_newPayloadWithWitness? (I'm not familiar with the engine api dos and donts)

author: developeruche
content: I don't think this is the case right now...

author: taulepton_
content: Ahh it only supports json-rpc?

author: developeruche
content: yeah... IPC support was enabled only for the normal external RPC calls (e.g the "eth_" namesapce)
